Objectives: First attempted lunar mission; test lunar orbit capabilities
Special Systems/Instruments: Thor-Able rocket
Notes: First US lunar attempt, exploded at T+77s

Objectives: First Soviet lunar attempt; impact the Moon
Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 ICBM derived launcher
Notes: Launch failure at T+92s

Special Systems/Instruments: Thor-Able rocket, magnetometer
Notes: Reached 113,800 km but fell back to Earth

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived launcher
Notes: Launch failure at T+104s

Special Systems/Instruments: Thor-Able rocket
Notes: Third stage failed to ignite

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived launcher
Notes: Launch failure at T+254s

Special Systems/Instruments: Juno II rocket, radiation detector
Notes: First stage shutdown early, reached 102,360 km

Objectives: First lunar impact attempt; measure radiation, magnetic fields
Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived, magnetometer, geiger counters, scintillation counters
Notes: Missed Moon by 5,995 km; first spacecraft to reach escape velocity; discovered solar wind

Special Systems/Instruments: Juno II, photoelectric sensor
Notes: Passed Moon at 60,000 km; returned radiation data; entered solar orbit

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived
Notes: Guidance system failure

Objectives: First spacecraft to impact the Moon; measure radiation, magnetic fields
Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived, magnetometer, geiger counters, micrometeorite detectors
Notes: FIRST spacecraft to reach surface of another celestial body; discovered no lunar magnetic field

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Able rocket
Notes: Payload shroud destroyed at T+45s

Objectives: First photographs of lunar far side
Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived, Yenisey-2 camera system, film return capsule
Notes: FIRST images of Moon's far side; photographed 70% of far side

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Able rocket, TV camera
Notes: Payload shroud failed, vehicle destroyed

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Able rocket
Notes: Second stage premature cutoff

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Able rocket
Notes: First stage explosion

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Agena B, TV system test
Notes: Agena restart failure, reentered Earth atmosphere

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Agena B
Notes: Agena restart failure, reentered Earth atmosphere

Objectives: Lunar impact; TV imaging until impact
Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Agena B, TV camera, gamma ray spectrometer, seismometer
Notes: Missed Moon by 36,800 km due to guidance error; entered solar orbit

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Agena B, TV camera, gamma ray spectrometer, seismometer
Notes: Impact on far side; no data returned due to computer/timer failure; first US spacecraft to reach Moon

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Agena B, TV camera, gamma ray spectrometer
Notes: Solar panel failed, missed Moon by 720 km

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived, landing system
Notes: Failed to leave Earth orbit

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived
Notes: Failed to leave Earth orbit

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived, landing system
Notes: Missed Moon by 8,336 km; entered solar orbit

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Agena B, 6 TV cameras
Notes: Impact in Sea of Tranquility; TV cameras failed, no images

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived
Notes: Launch vehicle failure

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived
Notes: Launch vehicle failure

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Agena B, 6 TV cameras (4 wide angle, 2 narrow)
Notes: FIRST US close-up images of Moon; 4,308 images returned; impacted in Mare Nubium

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Agena B, 6 TV cameras
Notes: 7,137 images returned; impacted in Mare Tranquillitatis (Apollo 11 landing site)

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Agena B, 6 TV cameras
Notes: 5,814 images returned; impacted in crater Alphonsus; first live TV from Moon

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived, landing system, TV camera
Notes: Retro-rockets failed; crashed in Mare Nubium

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived
Notes: Engine failed to shut off; missed Moon by 161,000 km

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived, Yenisey-2 camera system
Notes: 25 images of far side returned; entered solar orbit

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived, landing system
Notes: Retro-rockets fired too early; crashed in Oceanus Procellarum

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived, landing system
Notes: Retro-rockets fired too late; crashed in Oceanus Procellarum

Objectives: First soft landing on Moon; surface photography
Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived, airbag landing system, TV camera, radiation detector
Notes: FIRST soft landing on another celestial body; 3 panoramas from Oceanus Procellarum; proved surface could support weight

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived
Notes: Failed to leave Earth orbit

Objectives: First lunar orbiter; gamma ray spectroscopy
Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived, gamma ray spectrometer, magnetometer, meteoroid detector
Notes: FIRST spacecraft to orbit Moon; operated 56 days; detected mass concentrations (mascons)

Objectives: First US soft landing; surface photography
Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Centaur, TV camera, strain gauge landing sensor
Notes: FIRST US Moon landing; 11,240 images over 6 weeks; landed in Oceanus Procellarum

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived, gamma ray spectrometer, magnetometer
Notes: Operated 38 days in lunar orbit

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Centaur, TV camera
Notes: Vernier engine failed; crashed near Copernicus crater

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived, TV camera system, gamma ray spectrometer
Notes: 1,100 images returned; operated 86 days

Objectives: Lunar photography for Apollo site selection
Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Agena D, dual lens camera system (80mm/610mm)
Notes: First US lunar orbiter; 42 high-res and 187 medium-res images; first image of Earth from lunar orbit; intentional impact 10/29/1966

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Agena D, dual lens camera system
Notes: 800+ images including famous Copernicus oblique view; intentional impact 10/11/1967

Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived, 2 TV cameras, soil density meter, radiation densitometer, penetrometer
Notes: Landed in Oceanus Procellarum; soil density 0.8 g/cm3; 5 panoramas over 6 days

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Agena D, dual lens camera system
Notes: Camera failure after 75% of mission; 626 images returned

Objectives: Soft landing; surface sampling; Apollo site verification
Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Centaur, TV camera, surface sampler (scoop)
Notes: Landed in Oceanus Procellarum; first surface sampling; visited by Apollo 12 astronauts Nov 1969

Objectives: Global lunar mapping from polar orbit
Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Agena D, dual lens camera system
Notes: Camera failure after 2/3 of mission; first polar orbit; 419 images

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Centaur, TV camera, surface sampler
Notes: Radio contact lost 2.5 min before landing; likely exploded

Special Systems/Instruments: Delta E1, magnetometer, plasma probe, energetic particle detector
Notes: Operated 6 years from elliptical orbit

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Agena D, dual lens camera system
Notes: 212 images; mapped 99% of Moon; intentional impact 1/31/1968

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Centaur, TV camera, alpha scattering instrument (chemistry)
Notes: Landed in Mare Tranquillitatis; first in-situ chemical analysis; soil = basaltic rock

Objectives: Soft landing; surface chemistry; first lunar liftoff
Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Centaur, TV camera, alpha scattering instrument
Notes: Landed in Sinus Medii; first rocket launch from Moon surface (10 ft hop); 30,000 images

Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Centaur, TV camera, alpha scatterer, surface sampler, magnetometer
Notes: Landed near Tycho crater (highlands); 21,000 images; detected water ice hints in soil

Objectives: Lunar orbiter; communications test for crewed missions
Special Systems/Instruments: R-7 derived, radio equipment
Notes: Operated in lunar orbit; tested communications for Zond/crewed missions

Special Systems/Instruments: Proton rocket, Lunokhod rover, landing stage
Notes: Launch vehicle disintegrated at T+51s

Objectives: First crewed lunar orbit; Earthrise photo
Special Systems/Instruments: Saturn V, Command Module, Service Module; 3 astronauts (Borman, Lovell, Anders)
Notes: First humans to orbit Moon; 10 lunar orbits; iconic 'Earthrise' photograph; Christmas Eve broadcast

Objectives: Lunar module descent test; dress rehearsal for landing
Special Systems/Instruments: Saturn V, CM, SM, LM 'Snoopy'; 3 astronauts (Stafford, Young, Cernan)
Notes: LM descended to 15.6 km above surface; tested all systems for landing

Objectives: FIRST human Moon landing; sample return
Special Systems/Instruments: Saturn V, CM 'Columbia', LM 'Eagle'; Armstrong, Aldrin, Collins
Notes: FIRST humans on Moon; 'One small step'; 21.5 kg samples; Sea of Tranquillity; flag planted; seismic experiment

Special Systems/Instruments: Proton rocket, lander, ascent stage, return capsule
Notes: Crashed during descent in Mare Crisium while Apollo 11 was on surface

Special Systems/Instruments: Proton, Soyuz 7K-L1 spacecraft, biological payload (turtles)
Notes: Successful circumlunar return; 4 turtles survived; color photos of Earth and Moon

Objectives: Second human landing; precision landing; Surveyor 3 visit
Special Systems/Instruments: Saturn V, CM 'Yankee Clipper', LM 'Intrepid'; Conrad, Gordon, Bean
Notes: Pinpoint landing near Surveyor 3; 34 kg samples; first color TV from surface; retrieved Surveyor 3 parts

Special Systems/Instruments: Saturn V, CM, LM 'Aquarius'; Lovell, Swigert, Haise
Notes: Oxygen tank explosion; crew safely returned using LM as lifeboat; flew around Moon; 'successful failure'

Objectives: FIRST robotic sample return from beyond Earth
Special Systems/Instruments: Proton rocket, drill, sample return capsule; 101g returned
Notes: FIRST robotic sample return; 101g from Mare Fecunditatis; drilled 35cm deep

Special Systems/Instruments: Proton, Soyuz 7K-L1, biological payload
Notes: Successful circumlunar; turtles and flies survived; splashdown in Indian Ocean

Objectives: FIRST robotic rover on another world
Special Systems/Instruments: Proton rocket, Lunokhod 1 rover (756kg), TV cameras, X-ray spectrometer, penetrometer
Notes: Lunokhod 1: 322 days, 10.54 km, 20,000+ TV panoramas, 200+ soil analyses; landed in Mare Imbrium

Objectives: Third human landing; Fra Mauro formation
Special Systems/Instruments: Saturn V, CM 'Kitty Hawk', LM 'Antares'; Shepard, Mitchell, Roosa
Notes: First to hit golf balls on Moon; 43 kg samples; Fra Mauro formation; first use of MET (hand cart)

Objectives: Fourth human landing; FIRST Lunar Roving Vehicle; Hadley Rille
Special Systems/Instruments: Saturn V, CM 'Endeavour', LM 'Falcon', LRV; Scott, Irwin, Worden
Notes: FIRST LRV used; 27.9 km driven; 77 kg samples; Hadley-Apennine; first deep-space EVA (return); Genesis Rock

Special Systems/Instruments: Magnetometer, S-band transponder
Notes: Deployed by Apollo 15; studied lunar gravity and magnetism; crashed after 1 year

Special Systems/Instruments: Proton rocket, drill, return capsule
Notes: Crashed during landing in Mare Fecunditatis

Special Systems/Instruments: Proton rocket, gamma ray spectrometer, magnetometer, radar altimeter
Notes: Operated ~1 year; detailed gravity mapping

Special Systems/Instruments: Proton rocket, drill, return capsule; 55g returned
Notes: 55g samples from Apollonius highlands; similar to Luna 16 site

Special Systems/Instruments: Saturn V, CM 'Casper', LM 'Orion', LRV; Young, Duke, Mattingly
Notes: 26.9 km driven; 95.7 kg samples; Descartes Highlands; cosmic ray detector; first telescope on Moon (UV camera)

Special Systems/Instruments: Magnetometer, particle detectors
Notes: Orbit decayed prematurely due to lunar mascon influence

Special Systems/Instruments: N1 rocket, Soyuz 7K-LOK spacecraft
Notes: N1 first stage failure; launch pad destroyed

Objectives: Sixth and FINAL human Moon landing; Taurus-Littrow
Special Systems/Instruments: Saturn V, CM 'America', LM 'Challenger', LRV; Cernan, Schmitt (geologist), Evans
Notes: LAST human Moon landing (as of 2026); 35.7 km driven; 110.5 kg samples; ONLY scientist (geologist) on Moon; deepest drilling (3m); lunar rover max speed 17.7 km/h

Special Systems/Instruments: Proton rocket, Lunokhod 2 rover (840kg), 4 TV cameras, X-ray spectrometer, solar X-ray telescope
Notes: Lunokhod 2: 117 days, 39 km (LONGEST rover distance until Yutu-2), 86 panoramas; landed in Le Monnier crater

Special Systems/Instruments: Delta rocket, 230m V-shaped antenna, radio receivers (0.025-13.1 MHz)
Notes: Largest man-made antenna system; operated 4+ years in lunar orbit

Objectives: Lunar flyby en route to Mercury
Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas-Centaur, TV cameras, UV spectrometer
Notes: Lunar flyby for gravity assist; mapped north pole; continued to Mercury

Special Systems/Instruments: Proton rocket, imaging system, altimeter
Notes: Operated 18 months; detailed imaging from 30-200 km orbit

Special Systems/Instruments: Proton rocket, drill, return capsule
Notes: Landed but drill damaged on landing; no sample obtained

Special Systems/Instruments: Proton rocket, drill (2m depth), return capsule; 170.1g returned
Notes: 170.1g samples from Mare Crisium; drilled 2m deep; LAST Soviet/Russian Moon mission until Luna 25 (2023)

Objectives: Lunar flyby and orbiter; test technologies
Special Systems/Instruments: M-3SII rocket, dust counter, plasma instruments; first Japanese lunar mission
Notes: First Japanese lunar mission; first aerobraking into lunar orbit; intentional impact 4/10/1993

Special Systems/Instruments: Small orbiter
Notes: Communications failed after deployment; orbital insertion unconfirmed

Objectives: Lunar mapping; mineralogy; test sensor technologies
Special Systems/Instruments: Titan II rocket, UV/visible camera, near-IR camera, LIDAR, laser altimeter
Notes: Global mineralogical map; evidence of water ice at south pole; first digital lunar map

Special Systems/Instruments: Proton rocket, communications satellite
Notes: Failed GEO insertion; used Moon gravity assists to reach usable orbit; first commercial lunar flyby

Objectives: Lunar mapping; search for water ice; gravity field
Special Systems/Instruments: Athena II rocket, gamma ray spectrometer, neutron spectrometer, magnetometer, electron reflectometer, alpha particle spectrometer
Notes: Confirmed water ice at poles; global gravity map; intentional impact near south pole 7/31/1999 to search for water vapor (none detected)

Objectives: Lunar orbiter; test ion propulsion; mineralogical mapping
Special Systems/Instruments: Ariane 5, ion engine (Hall effect thruster), D-CIXS X-ray spectrometer, SIR infrared spectrometer, AMIE camera
Notes: FIRST European lunar mission; ion propulsion; smallest and cheapest lunar mission to date; intentional impact 9/3/2006

Objectives: India's first lunar mission; map surface; search for water; test impact
Special Systems/Instruments: PSLV rocket, 11 instruments (5 Indian + 6 international), Moon Impact Probe (MIP)
Notes: DISCOVERED water molecules on Moon (with NASA M3); 1,380 kg; MIP impacted near Shackleton crater; India's first deep space mission

Objectives: China's first lunar mission; comprehensive mapping
Special Systems/Instruments: Long March 3A, CCD stereo camera, laser altimeter, imaging spectrometer, gamma/X-ray spectrometers, microwave radiometer, high-energy particle detector, solar wind monitor
Notes: FIRST Chinese mission beyond Earth orbit; most complete lunar map at the time; intentional impact 3/1/2009

Objectives: High-resolution mapping; scout landing sites for Chang'e 3
Special Systems/Instruments: Long March 3C, CCD stereo camera (1.5m resolution), laser altimeter, gamma/X-ray spectrometers, microwave radiometer
Notes: 0.5m resolution images; left lunar orbit for L2 point; flew by asteroid Toutatis in 2012; now in solar orbit

Objectives: Japan's first major lunar mission; comprehensive study
Special Systems/Instruments: H-IIA rocket, main orbiter + 2 subsatellites (Okina & Ouna), terrain camera, multiband imager, spectral profiler, lunar radar sounder, laser altimeter, X-ray/gamma ray spectrometers, magnetic field sensor, charged particle sensor, plasma analyzer, radio science, HDTV camera
Notes: Most detailed gravity map until GRAIL; HDTV videos of Earthrise; confirmed mantle below crust; intentional impact 6/10/2009

Objectives: Communications relay for Kaguya far side
Special Systems/Instruments: Radio transponder
Notes: Relayed communications from Kaguya far side operations

Objectives: Very Long Baseline Interferometry for gravity mapping
Special Systems/Instruments: Radio sources for VLBI gravity mapping
Notes: Provided precise gravity field data when combined with Okina

Objectives: Search for water ice in permanently shadowed crater
Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas V, shepherding spacecraft + Centaur upper stage impactor, 9 science instruments (near-IR, visible, thermal cameras, spectrometers, photometer)
Notes: Impacted Cabeus crater near south pole; CONFIRMED water ice presence (~5% by weight); 24 gigatons of water estimated in crater

Objectives: Comprehensive lunar mapping; Apollo site imaging; resource identification; radiation environment
Special Systems/Instruments: Atlas V, LOLA (laser altimeter), LROC (cameras), LAMP (UV), Diviner (thermal), CRaTER (radiation), Mini-RF (radar), LEND (neutron detector)
Notes: Still operating as of 2026; mapped Apollo landing sites; identified water ice deposits; detailed topographic maps; critical for Artemis planning

Special Systems/Instruments: Same as orbiter mission
Notes: Intentionally impacted on Moon 3/1/2009 after orbital mission completion

Special Systems/Instruments: Delta II, magnetometers, electric field instruments, particle detectors
Notes: Originally Earth orbiter; moved to lunar orbit 2011; still studying lunar space environment

Special Systems/Instruments: Delta II, magnetometers, electric field instruments, particle detectors
Notes: Sister spacecraft to P1; both still active in lunar orbit

Special Systems/Instruments: Delta II, two spacecraft (Ebb & Flow), Ka-band ranging system, MoonKAM (education camera)
Notes: Highest resolution gravity map of Moon; revealed fractures in crust; intentional impact 12/17/2012

Objectives: Study lunar atmosphere and dust environment
Special Systems/Instruments: Minotaur V, NMS (neutral mass spectrometer), UVS (UV spectrometer), LDEX (dust detector), LLCD (laser comm demo)
Notes: Characterized lunar exosphere; LLCD demonstrated 622 Mbps laser communication; intentional impact 4/18/2014

Objectives: FIRST Chinese soft landing; FIRST lunar landing in 37 years; rover deployment
Special Systems/Instruments: Long March 3B, lander (3,780 kg), Yutu rover (140 kg), 8 instruments including UV telescope, EUV camera, ground-penetrating radar
Notes: FIRST soft landing since 1976; Yutu rover: 42 days mobile, 973 days total, 114.8m; lander still sending data as of 2026; discovered new basalt type

Objectives: FIRST landing on far side of Moon; rover exploration
Special Systems/Instruments: Long March 3B, lander, Yutu-2 rover (140 kg), low-frequency radio spectrometer, lunar neutron dosimeter, neutral atom detector; relay via Queqiao satellite
Notes: FIRST far side landing; Yutu-2: LONGEST operating lunar rover (7+ years), 1.455+ km; discovered gel-like substance; new mineral Changesite-(Y)

Objectives: Communications relay for Chang'e 4 far side mission
Special Systems/Instruments: Long March 4C, relay satellite at Earth-Moon L2 halo orbit, low-frequency radio astronomy instrument
Notes: FIRST relay satellite at Earth-Moon L2; enables far side communications; also conducting radio astronomy

Special Systems/Instruments: Small satellite, radio astronomy payload
Notes: Communications lost after launch

Objectives: Lunar orbiter; radio astronomy; amateur radio
Special Systems/Instruments: Small satellite (47 kg), radio astronomy, camera
Notes: Took first image of lunar far side and Earth together; crashed into Moon 7/31/2019

Objectives: FIRST Israeli lunar mission; FIRST privately funded lunar landing attempt
Special Systems/Instruments: Falcon 9, lander (150 kg), magnetometer, laser retroreflector, time capsule
Notes: Entered lunar orbit but crashed during landing due to main engine failure; first privately funded Moon mission

Objectives: India's second lunar mission; orbiter + lander/rover attempt
Special Systems/Instruments: GSLV Mk III, orbiter (2,379 kg) with 8 instruments; Vikram lander + Pragyan rover (lander failed)
Notes: Orbiter: 96% mission success; 8 instruments including OHRC (high-res camera), IIRS, CLASS; Vikram lander crashed 9/6/2019

Objectives: Soft landing attempt near south pole
Special Systems/Instruments: GSLV Mk III, Vikram lander (1,471 kg), Pragyan rover (27 kg), 4 instruments on lander, 2 on rover
Notes: Crashed 2.1 km from surface due to braking thruster anomaly; high-res images located debris

Special Systems/Instruments: 27 kg, APXS, LIBS instruments
Notes: Lost with Vikram lander crash

Special Systems/Instruments: Long March 5, orbiter + lander + ascender + returner (8.2t total); drill, scoop sampler, panorama camera, lunar mineral spectrometer, ground-penetrating radar
Notes: 1,731g samples returned from Mons Rumker (youngest lunar samples ever); FIRST new lunar samples since 1976; third country to achieve sample return

Objectives: Test Near Rectilinear Halo Orbit for Gateway
Special Systems/Instruments: Electron rocket, Lunar Photon stage, 12U CubeSat; C-band comm payload
Notes: FIRST spacecraft in NRHO; testing orbit for future Gateway station; still operating

Objectives: South Korea's FIRST lunar mission; technology demonstration
Special Systems/Instruments: Falcon 9, 678 kg orbiter, 6 payloads: LUTI (terrain camera), PolCam, ShadowCam (NASA), KMAG, KGRS, DTNPL; BLT trajectory
Notes: Extended mission to 2027; excellent observation capabilities; testing interplanetary internet

Objectives: FIRST Japanese private lunar landing; FIRST Arab lunar rover
Special Systems/Instruments: Falcon 9, Series 1 lander (340 kg), Rashid rover (10 kg), SORA-Q (JAXA/TOMY)
Notes: Software error caused altitude miscalculation; crashed from 5km; UAE's first lunar attempt

Objectives: Russia's FIRST lunar mission since 1976; south pole landing attempt
Special Systems/Instruments: Soyuz 2.1b, lander (1,750 kg), 8 instruments: ADRON-LR, ARIES-L, LASMA-LR, LIS-TV-RPM, PmL, THERMO-L, STS-L, MIDAS
Notes: Accelerometer unit failed; engines fired 127s instead of 84s; crashed near Boguslavsky crater; first Russian lunar attempt since 1976

Objectives: India's FIRST successful soft landing; FIRST near south pole landing
Special Systems/Instruments: GSLV Mk III, Vikram lander (1,749 kg), 4 instruments: ChaSTE, ILSA, RAMBHA-LP, LRU; lessons from Ch-2 failure incorporated
Notes: FIRST landing near lunar south pole (69.37°S); 4th country to soft-land; 'Vikram' means 'valor'

Objectives: FIRST successful Indian lunar rover; south pole exploration
Special Systems/Instruments: 26 kg rover, APXS (Alpha Particle X-ray Spectrometer), LIBS (Laser-Induced Breakdown Spectroscope)
Notes: 101.4m traverse; detected Sulfur, Al, Ca, Fe, Cr, Ti, Mn, Si, O; surface temp 70°C; slept during lunar night, didn't wake

Objectives: Japan's FIRST lunar landing; precision landing demonstration
Special Systems/Instruments: H-IIA rocket, SLIM lander (210 kg), LEV-1 rover (2.1 kg), LEV-2/SORA-Q (250g); pinpoint landing within 100m target
Notes: FIFTH country to soft-land; landed within 55m of target (pinpoint precision); tipped on nose; 2 rovers deployed; mission ended Aug 2024

Objectives: FIRST commercial soft landing on Moon; FIRST US landing since 1972
Special Systems/Instruments: Falcon 9, Nova-C lander (1,900 kg), 6 NASA CLPS payloads + 6 commercial payloads; methalox propulsion
Notes: Landed tilted ~30° at Malapert A (80.13°S); 6 NASA payloads operated; broke landing leg; first liquid methane/LOX landing on celestial body

Objectives: FIRST Vulcan Centaur launch; commercial lunar landing attempt
Special Systems/Instruments: Vulcan Centaur, Peregrine lander, 20 payloads (5 NASA CLPS + 15 commercial), 1.2t total
Notes: PCV2 valve failure caused propellant tank rupture; controlled reentry over South Pacific 1/18/2024

Objectives: FIRST samples from far side of Moon
Special Systems/Instruments: Long March 5, orbiter+lander+ascender+returner (8.35t); Queqiao-2 relay; 2kg samples; DORN (France), NILS (Sweden), INRRI; Jinchan rover
Notes: ~2kg samples from Apollo crater, SPA basin; FIRST far side samples; Jinchan rover (5kg) deployed; ICUBE-Q (Pakistan) cubesat

Objectives: Relay satellite for Chang'e 6 and future missions
Special Systems/Instruments: Long March 8, elliptical lunar orbit relay, extreme UV camera
Notes: Supports Chang'e 6, 7, 8 missions; communication relay for far side and polar regions

Special Systems/Instruments: CubeSat, camera, magnetometer (rideshare with Chang'e 6)
Notes: Released into lunar orbit; Pakistan's first lunar mission

Objectives: FIRST fully successful commercial Moon landing
Special Systems/Instruments: Falcon 9, Blue Ghost lander (1,517 kg), 10 NASA CLPS payloads, 94 kg science; Nammo LEROS engines
Notes: FIRST fully successful commercial landing; 119 GB data; 51 GB science; 5 hours into lunar night; lunar eclipse imagery; longest commercial surface ops

Objectives: Second Nova-C landing; south pole ice mining demo
Special Systems/Instruments: Falcon 9, Nova-C Athena (1,908 kg), PRIME-1 (TRIDENT drill + MSolo), μNova hopper, MAPP rover, Nokia 4G network
Notes: Landed on side in crater; incorrect orientation prevented rover deployment and some ops; 13 hours of operation; downlinked 4x IM-1 data

Objectives: Second ispace landing attempt; micro rover
Special Systems/Instruments: Falcon 9, Series 1 lander, TENACIOUS micro rover, UNESCO memory disk (275 languages)
Notes: Lost communication during descent; hard landing suspected in Mare Frigoris

Objectives: Search for water ice at south pole; ILRS precursor
Special Systems/Instruments: Orbiter + lander + rover + mini-flying probe; 18 science payloads; international partners (Egypt, Bahrain, Thailand, Russia, Italy, Switzerland); hyperspectral camera; Shackleton crater target
Notes: Will search for water ice in permanently shadowed regions; high-precision landing (<100m); before NASA VIPER

Special Systems/Instruments: Spee-3D Series 2 lander, 95 kg NASA PRISM payloads; 2 relay satellites (Blue Canyon); seismometers, heat flow probe, drill, magnetotelluric sounder
Notes: BOLDEST CLPS mission; only 2nd farside landing after Chang'e 4; study Moon's internal structure; prepare for Artemis

Objectives: Far side operations; Elytra Dark orbital vehicle
Special Systems/Instruments: Blue Ghost lander + Elytra Dark orbital vehicle; lunar orbit and far side surface operations
Notes: Annual lunar missions planned by Firefly; far side data collection

Special Systems/Instruments: Nova-C lander, NASA CLPS payloads; Reiner Gamma landing site (magnetic anomaly)
Notes: Reiner Gamma swirl region study

Special Systems/Instruments: Nova-C lander, 6 NASA science instruments; lunar south pole
Notes: Lunar south pole region exploration

Objectives: Large cargo lander; originally for VIPER
Special Systems/Instruments: Griffin lander (4,500 kg capacity), 14 engines; much larger than Peregrine
Notes: VIPER rover reassigned to Blue Origin; Griffin mission delayed pending redesign after Peregrine failure

Objectives: Search for water ice at lunar south pole
Special Systems/Instruments: Blue Moon MK1 lander, VIPER rover (450 kg); neutron spectrometer, NIR spectrometer, mass spectrometer, 1m drill; solar powered
Notes: CANCELLED July 2024, REVIVED Sept 2025; will map ice distribution; critical for Artemis crewed missions

Objectives: FIRST crewed lunar landing since Apollo 17
Special Systems/Instruments: SLS, Orion, Starship HLS; 2 astronauts to surface; SpaceX lander; new spacesuits (AxEMU); south pole region
Notes: FIRST woman and person of color on Moon; 6.5 days on surface; up to 4 EVAs; water ice search

Objectives: Second Artemis landing; Gateway first crew
Special Systems/Instruments: SLS, Orion, Starship HLS, Gateway (PPE+HALO); 4 astronauts; lunar surface + Gateway
Notes: FIRST Gateway crewed mission; new spacesuits; extended surface operations

Objectives: Third Artemis landing; Blue Origin lander debut
Special Systems/Instruments: SLS, Orion, Blue Moon HLS (Blue Origin); 4 astronauts; Lunar Terrain Vehicle
Notes: FIRST Blue Moon landing; LTV deployment; resource prospecting

Special Systems/Instruments: SLS, Orion, HLS, Gateway, LTV, habitation modules
Notes: Building lunar base camp; long-duration stays

Special Systems/Instruments: Power and Propulsion Element (PPE) + Habitation and Logistics Outpost (HALO); 40-50 kW power; ion propulsion; international partners
Notes: Critical for Artemis sustained presence; logistics hub; deep space research

Objectives: Lunar orbiter; map resources; scout landing sites
Special Systems/Instruments: 2,250 kg orbiter; gamma spectrometer, neutron detector, radar, terrain camera, plasma instruments; polar orbit 50-500 km
Notes: Replaces foreign components after Luna 25 failure; delayed from 2027; essential for future Russian landings

Objectives: Lunar south pole landing; drill for water ice
Special Systems/Instruments: 2,200 kg lander, 810 kg science payload; Luna-Resurs-1; drill, spectrometers, cameras; European LARA landing radar (now uncertain)
Notes: North pole landing; replace European components after sanctions

Special Systems/Instruments: Same configuration as Luna 27A; backup/complimentary mission
Notes: South pole landing; redundant approach after Luna 25 failure

Objectives: Frozen sample return from south pole
Special Systems/Instruments: 3,000 kg; lander + ascent vehicle + return capsule; miniature rover for sample selection; Luna-29 relay support
Notes: Frozen samples to preserve volatiles; most ambitious Russian lunar mission

Special Systems/Instruments: Relay orbiter for Luna 28 and Luna 30 surface missions; communications infrastructure
Notes: Critical communications backbone for future Russian surface missions

Special Systems/Instruments: 3,000 kg lander + heavy rover; long-range mobility; extensive instrumentation
Notes: Most ambitious Russian lunar surface mission; heavy rover for extensive exploration

Special Systems/Instruments: 5 spacecraft: propulsion + descender + ascender + transfer + re-entry module; 2 launches (LVM-3 + PSLV); docking in lunar orbit (SPADEX test)
Notes: Will join US, USSR, China in sample return capability; most complex ISRO mission to date

Special Systems/Instruments: Design complete; objectives undisclosed
Notes: Follow-on to Chandrayaan-4

Objectives: In-situ resource utilization testing; 3D printing with lunar soil
Special Systems/Instruments: Lander, rover, hopper, robots; 3D printing demo; closed ecosystem experiment; power/beam communication; 11 international partners including Pakistan (rover), Türkey
